Vol. I · No. 1

Lexical
Soundings

A phoneme & grapheme
lookup, by way of
CMU Pronouncing Dict.
Variant
Notation
Stress
loading dictionary…

Type any English word to see its phonemic transcription. Multiple pronunciations are listed where the dictionary records them. Words like cough, read, and bass have more than one.

Reverse lookup: enter a sequence of phonemes and find every word pronounced that way. A homophone finder, essentially. Use spaces between phonemes — e.g. T R AO F for trough, or in IPA t r ɔ f.

Find words by partial match — either by spelling (graphemes) or by sound (phonemes). Useful for "all words ending in -ough" or "all words containing the cluster /STR/".

Rhymes are matched on the rime of the stressed syllable — every phoneme from the last primary-stressed vowel onward. Perfect rhymes share that whole tail; near rhymes share only the final consonant pattern.

Pick a palette of sounds, letter sequences, or both — then find words that fit. Must include all finds words containing every selected item. Built only from finds words composed entirely of selected items, the way a phonics teacher checks which words a student can read given a set of taught correspondences.

US pronunciations from the CMU Pronouncing Dictionary (public domain). UK pronunciations from Britfone (MIT, RP / Standard Southern British).
